rulings.Case Title: LEGAL RECOVERY, LLC VS. MARTIN LEE ENG
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion For Sanctions
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Monday, January 7, 2019, Line 3, PLAINTIFF LEGAL RECOVERY, LLC's Motion For Sanctions Plaintiff Legal Recovery, LLC's motion for sanctions pursuant to CCP 128.7 is granted in large part. The cross-complaint filed by Lombard Flats, LLC is stricken as a sanction because there is no factual or legal support for the cross-complaint which is an impermissible collateral attack on a judgment by a party who is not a judgment debtor and lacks standing to vacate the judgment. No monetary sanctions are awarded in the court's discretion because there has been no showing that either Lombard Flats or its attorney filed the cross-complaint in subjective bad faith and the striking the cross-complaint is sufficient to remedy the violation of CCP 128.7(b)(2) and (3). Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for Legal Recovery is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =302/hk

rulings.Case Title: BB/CC, A CALIFORNIA JOINT VENTURE AKA BALFOUR VS. CORTE CLADDING CONSULTANS
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Plaintiff To Provide Further Responses To Written Discovery
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Monday, January 7, 2019, Line 1, DEFENDANT CURTAIN WALL DESIGN AND CONSULTING, INC. Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Plaintiff To Provide Further Responses To Written Discovery (Part 1 of 2) Pro Tem Judge Scott Borrowman, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Discovery may be had "if the matter either is itself admissible in evidence or appears reasonably calculated to lead to the discovery of admissible evidence." CCP 2017.010. BB/CC objects CDC's requested discovery is: not permitted by CCP 2017.210, not relevant, and an attempt to sidestep the collateral source rule and discover prejudicial information. Evidence of liability insurance often is not relevant to the underlying claims and so is not usually admissible. CCP 2017.210 "allows limited discovery of a defendant's liability insurance coverage as a matter of right; that is to say, without the need for a threshold showing of relevancy and admissibility" under CCP 2017.010. Catholic Mut. Relief Soc. v. Sup. Ct. (2007) 42 Cal.4th 358, 366-67. As it does not appear Steadfast would be liable to satisfy a judgment or to indemnify BB/CC, CCP 2017.210 is inapplicable. Attribution of responsibility for delays is relevant. BB/CC gave Steadfast "support documents" about cost overruns and delays by Zetian and other subcontractors (including presumably CDC). (Gibson Ex 7/Thompson Dec 7). Thus, there are questions whether, and if yes, how and as to what timeframes, BB/CC attributed responsibility among the subcontractors. BB/CC argues its claims against CDC postdate Zetian's work on the Project but without the discovery sought CDC cannot test that assertion. This is sufficient for discovery under CCP 2017.010. Whether the evidence will be inadmissible as a collateral source, or as more prejudicial than probative, are questions about admissibility not discoverability. Williams v. Sup. Ct. (2017), 3 Cal.5th 531, 541 (discovery not limited to admissible evidence). The motion is GRANTED as to RFPs 25-31, 35-37, 44, and Rogs 39-41, 45-56. The motion is DENIED as to RFPs 32-34, which relate to payment and not attribution. The motion is DENIED as to the other Rogs because "SUBGUARD POLICY" is not defined. CCP 2030.060(e). Any production may include appropriate confidentiality legends. Sanctions in favor of CDC in the amount of $3,760. CCP 2030.300(d), 2031.310(h). (end part 1 of 2)

rulings.Case Title: ALBERT SHIH ET AL VS. YI TAN ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION / Notice Of Motion & Motion For Reconsideration Of Order Granting Defendants' Motion To Vacate Default & Default Judgment; Request For Fees And Costs
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Monday, January 7, 2019, Line 5, PLAINTIFFs ALBERT SHIH and DANNY WEN's MOTION /Motion For Reconsideration Of Order Granting Defendants' Motion To Vacate Default & Default Judgment; Request For Fees And Costs Plaintiffs Albert Shih and Danny Wen's motion for reconsideration of order granting defendant Yi Tan's motion to vacate default and default judgment is continued to February 7, 2019 at 2pm in department 302. The court exercises its discretion per Jay v. Mahaffey (2013) 218 Cal. App.4th 1522 to consider the evidence submitted with the reply papers. Per Jay, Mr. Tan may file supplemental opposition papers directed to the reply evidence no later than January 30, 2019. The court has further determined that brief (likely no more than an hour) of live testimony of Mr. Tan would assist in the adjudication of this motion. Mr. Tan must appear in person or by live video such as FaceTime or Skype at the hearing on February 7. Counsel for plaintiffs and counsel for Mr. Tan will each be afforded an opportunity to ask questions of Mr. Tan and the court may also ask some questions of Mr. Tan. If counsel and the parties are not available on February 7, counsel should meet and confer and notify the court by email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org of at least two dates that are agreeable to the parties. =302/hk

rulings.Case Title: **TRANSFERRED TO LOS ANGELES ** NICOLE TATE-NAGHI VS. STEVEN R FRIEDMAN
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION TO TRANSFER
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Monday, January 7, 2019, Line 6, DEFENDANTs STEVEN FRIEDMAN and MICHAEL FRIEDMAN's LAW OFFICES OF STEVEN FRIEDMAN MOTION TO TRANSFER Defendants Steven Friedman, Michael Friedman, and Law Offices of Steven Friedman's motion to transfer venue to Los Angeles County is granted. This case is transferred to central district of Los Angeles County Superior Court and all transfer costs must be paid by plaintiff Nicole Tate-Naghi. Venue is presumptively proper in the county where any of the defendants resides. (CCP 395(a).) The first amended complaint alleges that all of the defendants reside in Los Angeles County. Ms. Tate-Naghi has alleged transitory causes of action for professional negligence and breach of fiduciary duty that do not involve the internal affairs of the trust. The conversion cause of action merely asserts that these alleged breaches of professional and fiduciary duties harmed the trust, which does not implicate the internal affairs of the trust. Probate Code sections 17000 and 17005 are inapplicable and Ms. Tate-Naghi's arguments that this case should remain with the two trust cases in San Francisco lack merit. Transferring this case to Los Angeles is not inconsistent with the decision of the Los Angeles Superior Court transferring the probate case to San Francisco because this case is not a probate division case and does not implicate the internal affairs of the trust. Furthermore, Haines v. Lamb (1962) 206 Cal.App.2d 322 and CCP 395.1 are inapplicable because this case is not a lawsuit against an executor, administrator, guardian, conservator, or trustee. Defendants' request for fees and expenses per CCP 396b(b) is denied in the exercise of the court's discretion because Ms. Tate-Naghi had a good faith, albeit mistaken, basis for filing this case in San Francisco and her untimely filed opposition papers did not unfairly prejudice the defendants. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for defendants is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portions of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =302/hk

rulings.Case Title: MAD DOG IN THE CITY, INC. VS. ALJERIE KATE M. CASTILLA ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion; Memorandum Of Points And Authorities; And Declaration Of Tara J. Macomber In Support Of Motion To Compel Responses To Requests For Production Of Documents; Set One, And Monetary Sanctions
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Monday, January 7, 2019, Line 3, DEFENDANT ALJERIE CASTILLA'S Motion To Compel Responses To Requests For Production Of Documents; Set One, And Monetary Sanctions. Pro Tem Judge Scott Borrowman, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: From the Opposition and Reply it appears the requested documents were produced after the motion was filed, so the motion to compel is moot. Under CRC 3.1348, the Court may approve sanctions where the requested discovery was provided after the motion was filed. According to the declarations, counsel for movant agreed to accept service of documents not subject to confidentiality concerns by 12/6/2018. The documents were not served until 12/12/2018, were served by mail, and were not received by the time the present motion was filed. These factors weigh in favor of sanctions. On the other hand, counsel for movant could have contacted opposing counsel to confirm whether production had been made before spending time preparing the motion. On balance, the award of attorney fees is unwarranted because of a failure to meet-and-confer before filing this motion. However, it is appropriate to require reimbursement of the filing fee. The Court awards sanctions of $60 in favor of movant. CCP 2031.300(c), CRC 3.1348. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to sborrowman@gmail.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: GEODE INVESTMENTS PRIME, LLC ET AL VS. SEAN SHARMA ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Deposition Of Person Most Qualified To Testify On Behalf Of Defendant Ultratick, Inc. And For Monetary Sanctions
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Monday, January 7, 2019, Line 5, PLAINTIFFS GEODE INVESTMENTS PRIME, LLC, GEODE INVESTMENTS BETA, LLC'S Motion To Compel Deposition Of Person Most Qualified To Testify On Behalf Of Defendant Ultratick, Inc. And For Monetary Sanctions. Pro Tem Judge Scott Borrowman, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: From the moving and opposition papers it appears that Defendants agreed to make the corporate representative available for deposition, and that Defendants did not object to the topics in the original or revised notices of deposition. As such, the only dispute appears to be about the date of the deposition. The meet-and-confer correspondence indicates that Defendants made it clear that the corporate representative was unavailable on December 10, 2018 (Levine Ex B), so it was unreasonable to take a no-show on that date. The meet-and-confer correspondence further indicates that Defendants made it clear that the corporate representative was unavailable through mid-January (Levine Ex B). This is sufficient to deny the motion to compel the deposition to occur the week of January 7. An additional factor supports denial of the motion. As discussed in Levine Ex C, it appears that Plaintiffs have refused (at least as of the filing of the Opposition) to provide dates for deposition of their witnesses. The Court is disinclined to sanction one party when the other party allegedly has engaged in similar conduct. The motion is denied. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to sborrowman@gmail.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: LEERINK REVELATION HEALTHCARE FUND I, L.P ET AL VS. BALAJI K SRINIVASAN ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: DEMURRER TO CROSS COMPLAINT
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Monday, January 7, 2019, Line 10, CROSS DEFENDANTs LEERINK REVELATION HEALTHCARE FUND I, L.P, LEERINK REVELATION HEALTHCARE FUND II, L.P DEMURRER TO CROSS COMPLAINT Cross-defendants Leerink Revelation Healthcare Fund I, L.P and Leerink Revelation Healthcare Fund II, L.P's demurrer to the sole cause of action for fraud in the inducement in the cross-complaint filed by defendant and cross-claimant Balaji Srinivasan is overruled. California substantive law, not Delaware substantive law, governs the issue of the sufficiency of the reliance allegations in Mr. Srinivasan's fraud claim. Per Ron Greenspan Volkswagen, Inc. v. Ford Motor Land Development Corp. (1995) 32 Cal.App.4th 985, Mr. Srinivasan has adequately alleged that he relied on the alleged fraud of the Leerink entities. California law as set forth in Ron Greenspan conflicts with Delaware law on the legal effect of anti-reliance clauses. California has a materially greater interest than Delaware in the issue of whether the anti-reliance contractual clause bars Mr. Srinivasan's fraud claim. That issue does not involve the internal affairs of Delaware corporate citizens. California's extremely strong interest in preventing the enforcement of a contract exempting a California resident corporation from responsibility for fraudulently inducing a contract in California with a California resident that was substantially performed in California is far greater than "Delaware's general, though profound, interest in vindicating freedom of contract." (EBP Lifestyle Brands Holdings, Inc. v. Boulbain (Del. Ch., Aug. 4, 2017) 2017 WL 3328363 at *8). Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for Mr. Srinivasan is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =302/hk

rulings.Case Title: MAURICE SULLIVAN ET AL VS. TERRY D. SULLIVAN GREEN ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: DEMURRER TO COMPLAINT
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Monday, January 7, 2019, Line 11, DEFENDANT TERRY SULLIVAN-GREEN's DEMURRER TO COMPLAINT Defendant Terry Sullivan-Green's demurrer to both causes of action in the complaint is overruled as to both causes of action. Neither of the two claims are a "trust contest" per the Probate Code because plaintiffs are neither trustees nor beneficiaries under the restatement of the trust. Therefore, the probate division does not have exclusive jurisdiction of this case and the limitations period of Probate Code 16061.8 is inapplicable to the two claims alleged by plaintiffs. The second cause of action for intentional interference with expected inheritance is adequately alleged per Beckwith v. Dahl (2012) 205 Cal.App.4th 1039. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for plaintiffs is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =302/hk

rulings.Case Title: LUCILA MEDINA GONZALEZ VS. AMERICA'S WHOLE SALE LENDER AS ORIGINAL LENDER ET AL

rulings.Case Title: YVONNE DETERT ET AL VS. CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: DEMURRER TO COMPLAINT
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Monday, January 7, 2019, Line 14, DEFENDANTs CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO and JOSE CISNEROS DEMURRER TO COMPLAINT Defendants City and County of San Francisco and Jose Cisneros' demurrer to both causes of action in the complaint is overruled as to both causes of action. Except for deductions, exemptions, and credits, tax statutes are strictly construed in favor of the taxpayer. (Taiheyo Cement U.S.A., Inc. v. Franchise Tax Board (2012) 204 Cal. App. 4th 254). The City's argument that the waiver of penalty provision in SF Business and Tax Regulations Code 6.17-4 should not be construed in plaintiffs' favor because that provision waives a penalty and does not impose a tax lacks merit. The City makes a distinction without a difference and cases that interpret tax credit statutes are inapposite. Section 6.17-4 provides that a penalty may be waived where the failure to make a timely payment occurred "notwithstanding the exercise of ordinary care by the taxpayer and in the absence of wilful neglect." Plaintiffs allege sufficient ultimate facts showing that they exercised ordinary care, there was no wilful neglect, and they are entitled to a waiver. United State v. Boyle (1985) 469 U.S. 241 and its progeny are inapposite. Those cases interpreted the "reasonable cause" standard for a penalty waiver for the federal Internal Revenue Code. Section 6.17-4 no longer includes such "reasonable cause" language. The 2004 version included the phrase "reasonable cause," but it was subsequently amended to include the "ordinary care" language. The amendment had significance. "In general, a substantial change in the language of a statute ... by an amendment indicates an intention to change its meaning. It is presumed the Legislature made changes in wording and phraseology deliberately and intended different meanings when using different words." (In re Marriage of Duffy (2001) 91 Cal.App.4th 923, 939(internal quotation marks and citations omitted)). Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for plaintiffs is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =302/hk
